name: Regulation & capital
description: The Basel framework: how much capital and liquidity a bank must hold.
primer:
The **Basel Accords** are international banking standards from the Basel Committee, transposed into national law (EU CRR/CRD, US rules): **Basel I** (1988) → **II** (2004) → **2.5** (2009) → **III** (2010–, with 2017 'Basel IV' finalisation).

**Evolution.** *Basel I*: minimum **8%** capital ratio against crudely risk-weighted assets (0/20/50/100% weights), almost all credit risk — invited arbitrage. *Basel II*: risk-sensitive capital via internal models, the *three pillars*, an explicit op-risk charge (criticised post-2008 for procyclicality and model reliance). *Basel 2.5*: trading-book fixes — stressed VaR, incremental risk charge, securitisation penalties. *Basel III*: more and higher-quality capital, leverage + liquidity (LCR, NSFR), buffers, systemic surcharges; its 2017 elements (output floor, revised approaches) are 'Basel IV'.

**Three Pillars.** P1 = minimum capital by formula; P2 = supervisory review (**ICAAP** by the bank, **SREP** by the supervisor) for risks P1 misses (concentration, IRRBB), with add-ons; P3 = standardised public disclosure for market discipline.

**RWA & ratios.** **RWA** $=\\sum_i\\mathrm{EAD}_i\\times w_i$ — riskier assets consume more capital; it is the denominator of every ratio. *Capital adequacy ratio* $\\mathrm{CAR}=\\frac{\\text{eligible capital}}{\\mathrm{RWA}}\\ge 8\\%$. *RWA density* = RWA/total assets (gauges average riskiness; low density invites scrutiny).

**Capital tiers (quality ladder).** **CET1** (common shares + retained earnings) = highest quality, *going-concern* loss absorption, $\\ge 4.5\\%$ of RWA. **Tier 1** = CET1 + Additional Tier 1 (AT1 'CoCos' convert/write down at a trigger), going-concern, $\\ge 6\\%$. **Tier 2** = mainly subordinated debt, *gone-concern* — absorbs losses only in **liquidation**; Total (T1+T2) $\\ge 8\\%$.

**Backstops & buffers.** *Leverage ratio* = Tier 1 / total exposure (non-risk-weighted), $\\ge\\mathbf{3\\%}$ — guards against gaming risk weights. *Capital conservation buffer* = **2.5%** CET1 above minimums; sitting inside it triggers automatic limits on dividends/bonuses (it doesn't fail the bank). *Countercyclical buffer (CCyB)* = variable CET1 (0–2.5%+), **raised in booms, released in downturns**, set by national authorities. *G-SIB surcharge* = extra CET1 scaled by systemic footprint (size, interconnectedness, complexity, cross-jurisdictional, substitutability). *Pillar 1 minimums*: 4.5% CET1, 6% T1, 8% total, 3% leverage, 100% LCR/NSFR — buffers stack on top; falling into a buffer restricts payouts, breaching a minimum triggers resolution.

**Approaches.** *Standardised (SA)*: regulator-set weights keyed to external ratings — simple, comparable. *Internal ratings-based (IRB)*: own PD (Foundation), plus own LGD/EAD (Advanced), constrained by input and output floors. The **output floor** caps IRB benefit: model RWA $\\ge\\mathbf{72.5\\%}$ of the standardised figure (the headline 'Basel IV' constraint). **FRTB** redesigns market risk: ES at 97.5% with liquidity horizons, a harder trading/banking-book boundary. FRTB *SBM* (standardised) = delta + vega + curvature + DRC + RRAO (mandatory even for IMA banks). FRTB *IMA* (internal) = ES with desk-level P&L-attribution and backtesting approval; non-modellable risk factors get a punitive stressed charge.

**Liquidity & resolution.** *LCR* = HQLA / 30-day stressed net outflows $\\ge 100\\%$ (survive **30** days). *NSFR* = available / required stable funding over **1** year $\\ge 100\\%$ (structural funding). *CVA charge*: capital for MtM losses from counterparties' credit-quality deterioration on derivatives (separate from default risk). *TLAC/MREL*: minimum loss-absorbing + recapitalisation capacity (equity + bail-inable debt) so banks are resolved by bail-in, not bailout (TLAC = FSB global; MREL = EU). *IFRS 9 ECL*: forward-looking provisioning, Stage 1 = 12-month ECL, Stages 2–3 = lifetime.

cards.definition: The series of international banking standards issued by the Basel Committee, harmonising capital, liquidity and risk rules across jurisdictions. Not law themselves, they are transposed into national regulation (e.g. EU CRR/CRD, US rules).
cards.note: Basel I (1988) → II (2004) → 2.5 (2009) → III (2010–) → 'Basel IV' finalisation.

cards.term: Basel I (1988) set a minimum {{8}}% capital ratio against crudely risk-weighted assets, focused almost entirely on credit risk.
cards.definition: The 1988 accord introducing a minimum 8% capital ratio against crudely risk-weighted assets, focused almost entirely on credit risk. Its broad-brush weights (0/20/50/100%) invited regulatory arbitrage but established the capital-ratio paradigm.
cards.note: A mortgage and a junk loan could attract the same weight — the flaw II addressed.

cards.definition: The 2004 framework introducing risk-sensitive capital via internal models, the three-pillar structure, and an explicit operational-risk charge. It let sophisticated banks use their own (IRB) estimates, increasing sensitivity but also model dependence.
cards.note: Criticised post-2008 for procyclicality and over-reliance on bank models.

cards.definition: The 2009 enhancement to trading-book rules after the crisis, adding stressed VaR, an incremental risk charge and securitisation penalties. A stopgap that roughly tripled some trading-book capital before the fuller FRTB rewrite.
cards.note: Bolted fixes onto VaR rather than redesigning the regime.

cards.definition: The post-crisis overhaul raising the quantity and quality of capital, adding leverage and liquidity (LCR, NSFR) requirements, buffers, and systemic surcharges. Its final 2017 elements — the output floor and revised approaches — are often called 'Basel IV'.
cards.note: Shifted emphasis to loss-absorbing common equity, leverage and liquidity together.

cards.definition: Basel II's architecture: Pillar 1 sets minimum capital by formula; Pillar 2 is supervisory review of risks not fully captured; Pillar 3 mandates market disclosure to enforce discipline. The pillars are complementary, not alternatives.
cards.note: Pillar 1 is the floor; Pillar 2 catches what the formulas miss.

cards.definition: The supervisory pillar requiring banks to assess all material risks and capital adequacy (ICAAP), which supervisors then evaluate and challenge (SREP), often imposing add-ons. It captures concentration, IRRBB and other risks outside Pillar 1.
cards.note: Where idiosyncratic, judgement-based capital requirements are set.

cards.definition: The market-discipline pillar mandating standardised public disclosure of risk, capital and RWA so that investors and counterparties can assess a bank. Transparency is meant to reward prudence and penalise opacity.
cards.note: Comparability across banks is the design goal.

cards.definition: Exposures scaled by risk weights so that riskier assets consume more capital, the denominator of every capital ratio. Computing RWA via standardised or internal-model approaches is the central machinery of the framework.
The ratio of eligible regulatory capital to risk-weighted assets, with an 8% Pillar 1 minimum before buffers. The headline solvency gauge of the Basel framework.

The highest-quality capital — common shares and retained earnings — that absorbs losses on a going-concern basis. The minimum CET1 ratio is 4.5% of RWA before the conservation and other buffers stack on top.

Going-concern capital comprising CET1 plus Additional Tier 1 (e.g. perpetual instruments that convert or write down under stress). It must be at least 6% of RWA and absorbs losses while the bank keeps operating.
cards.note: AT1 'CoCos' convert to equity or write down when CET1 falls below a trigger.

cards.definition: Gone-concern capital — mainly subordinated debt — that absorbs losses only in liquidation, protecting depositors and senior creditors. Total capital (Tier 1 + Tier 2) must be at least 8% of RWA.
cards.note: Lower quality than Tier 1: it cushions failure, not survival.

cards.term: The Basel III leverage ratio is a non-risk-weighted backstop — Tier 1 capital over a total exposure measure — with a {{3}}% minimum.
cards.definition: A non-risk-weighted backstop: Tier 1 capital over a total exposure measure including off-balance-sheet items, with a 3% minimum. It guards against gaming of risk weights and against model error in RWA.
cards.note: Binds when RWA-based requirements look deceptively low.

cards.term: The capital conservation buffer is a {{2.5}}% CET1 buffer above the minimums; sitting inside it triggers automatic limits on dividends and bonuses.
cards.definition: A 2.5% CET1 buffer above minimums that a bank may draw down in stress, but at the cost of automatic restrictions on dividends and bonuses. It builds usable capital in good times.
cards.note: Breaching it doesn't fail the bank — it constrains payouts to rebuild capital.

cards.definition: A variable CET1 buffer (0–2.5%+) that authorities raise in credit booms and release in downturns, leaning against procyclicality. It aims to build defences when systemic risk is accumulating.
cards.note: A macroprudential dial, set by national authorities on domestic exposures.

cards.definition: An additional CET1 requirement on global systemically important banks, scaled by their systemic footprint (size, interconnectedness, complexity, cross-jurisdictional activity, substitutability). It internalises the externality that a giant's failure imposes on the system.
cards.note: Higher buckets demand higher surcharges — a tax on systemic importance.

cards.definition: Capital computed with regulator-set risk weights keyed to external ratings or exposure type, requiring no internal model. Simpler and more comparable across banks, at the cost of risk sensitivity.
cards.note: The revised SA reduces mechanical reliance on external ratings.

cards.definition: An approach letting approved banks use their own PD (and, under advanced IRB, LGD and EAD) estimates in supervisory capital formulas. More risk-sensitive but model-dependent, and now constrained by input floors and the output floor.
cards.note: Foundation IRB: own PD only; Advanced IRB: own PD, LGD, EAD.

cards.sub: Fundamental Review of the Trading Book
cards.definition: The redesigned market-risk framework replacing VaR with expected shortfall, hardening the boundary between trading and banking books, and overhauling both standardised and internal-model approaches. The most significant market-risk reform since 1996.
cards.note: Switches the regulatory measure to 97.5% ES with liquidity horizons.

cards.definition: A sensitivities-based method summing delta, vega and curvature charges across prescribed risk classes with regulatory correlations, plus default and residual add-ons. Now mandatory to compute even for internal-model banks, as a floor and fallback.
cards.formula: capital $=$ SBM (delta + vega + curvature) $+$ DRC $+$ RRAO

cards.definition: The model-based FRTB option using expected shortfall with liquidity horizons, subject to desk-level approval via a P&L attribution test and backtesting. Desks failing the tests are forced onto the standardised approach.
cards.note: Non-modellable risk factors get a separate, punitive stressed-scenario charge.

cards.term: The Liquidity Coverage Ratio requires enough high-quality liquid assets to survive {{30}} days of stressed net cash outflows.
cards.definition: A Basel III rule requiring enough high-quality liquid assets to cover 30 days of stressed net cash outflows. It ensures a bank can survive a month-long liquidity shock unaided.

cards.term: The Net Stable Funding Ratio requires available stable funding to cover required stable funding over a {{1}}-year horizon.
cards.definition: A Basel III rule requiring available stable funding to cover required stable funding over a one-year horizon, curbing reliance on short-term wholesale money. It targets structural, not acute, liquidity risk — the complement to the LCR.

cards.term: The Basel III output floor stops internal-model RWA from falling below {{72.5}}% of the standardised figure.
cards.definition: A Basel III backstop capping the benefit of internal models: model-based RWA cannot fall below 72.5% of the standardised result. It limits variability and gaming of internal-model capital across banks.
cards.note: The headline 'Basel IV' constraint, phased in gradually.

cards.definition: Capital held against the risk of mark-to-market losses from deterioration in counterparties' credit quality on derivative exposures. Introduced after CVA losses, not outright defaults, drove much of 2008's derivative damage.
cards.note: Counterparty default risk and CVA (spread) risk are capitalised separately.

cards.definition: Requirements that systemic banks hold a minimum of loss-absorbing and recapitalisation capacity (equity plus bail-inable debt) to be resolved without taxpayer support. They make bail-in, not bailout, the default failure path.
cards.note: TLAC is the FSB global standard; MREL the EU equivalent.

cards.definition: The accounting standard requiring forward-looking expected-loss provisioning, staged by whether credit risk has significantly increased since origination. It front-loads loss recognition versus the old incurred-loss model.
cards.note: Stage 1 books 12-month ECL; stages 2–3 book lifetime ECL.

cards.definition: The ratio of risk-weighted assets to total assets — a quick gauge of average portfolio riskiness and of how aggressively internal models are reducing capital. Suspiciously low density invites supervisory scrutiny.

The hard Basel III floors before buffers: 4.5% CET1, 6% Tier 1 and 8% total capital of RWA, plus a 3% leverage ratio and 100% LCR/NSFR. Buffers (conservation, countercyclical, systemic) stack on top of these minimums.
cards.note: Falling into the buffer triggers payout restrictions; breaching the minimum triggers resolution.

cards.term: Tier 2 capital absorbs losses on a going-concern basis.
cards.summary: False — Tier 2 is gone-concern; it mainly absorbs losses in liquidation.
cards.definition: False. Tier 2 (mainly subordinated debt) is gone-concern capital that absorbs losses in liquidation, protecting depositors and senior creditors. Going-concern loss absorption is CET1 and Additional Tier 1.
cards.note: [1] Basel III — https://www.bis.org/

cards.term: The countercyclical capital buffer is raised during downturns and released during credit booms.
cards.summary: False — it's the other way round: raised in booms, released in downturns.
cards.definition: False. The countercyclical buffer (0–2.5%+ CET1) is built up in credit booms and released in downturns, leaning against procyclicality so banks keep lending in a bust.
cards.note: [1] Basel III — https://www.bis.org/

cards.term: CET1 is the highest-quality, going-concern loss-absorbing capital.
cards.summary: True — common shares plus retained earnings, absorbing losses while the bank operates.
cards.definition: True. Common Equity Tier 1 — common shares and retained earnings — is the highest-quality capital and absorbs losses on a going-concern basis; its Pillar 1 minimum is 4.5% of RWA.
cards.note: [1] Basel III — https://www.bis.org/
